title: Yudhishthira's Half-Truth
subtitle: Ashwatthama hata
context: To disarm the invincible Drona, the Pandavas announced 'Ashwatthama is dead'—true only of an elephant of that name. Yudhishthira muttered the qualifier. Drona laid down his arms and was slain, and Yudhishthira's chariot, long held aloft by his truth, sank to the earth.
dilemma: Does a half-truth told for a righteous cause stay a lie?
cutsBothWays: Defends a noble end can justify decisive, hard action. Warns one lapse erodes a lifetime of earned integrity.
modernParallel: An officer who fudges a single statistic to make a flagship scheme look successful.
quoteReady: "Even a sliver of falsehood grounded the chariot of truth."

title: Arjuna's Vishada
subtitle: Nishkama Karma
context: On the battlefield, Arjuna collapses in despair, unwilling to fight his own kin. Krishna counsels him to perform his rightful duty with courage and skill, but without attachment to victory, spoils, or praise—action offered up, not clung to.
dilemma: Do we act for the work itself, or for what we will get from it?
cutsBothWays: Defends duty performed selflessly is the highest integrity. Warns 'detachment' can become an excuse for cold indifference.
modernParallel: An officer who delivers relentlessly yet never lobbies for credit, postings, or awards.
quoteReady: "Your right is to the action, never to its fruit."

title: Karna's Loyalty
subtitle: The loyal outsider
context: Born to Kunti yet raised by a charioteer, Karna is spurned by society and embraced only by Duryodhana. Out of gratitude, he stands by his friend to the end, even knowing the cause is unjust and the Pandavas are his own brothers.
dilemma: When loyalty to a benefactor collides with what is right, which wins?
cutsBothWays: Defends gratitude and steadfast loyalty are noble virtues. Warns personal loyalty must not eclipse a higher duty to justice.
modernParallel: An official who shields a mentor's wrongdoing out of personal debt.
quoteReady: "Gratitude is a virtue — until it is asked to defend a wrong."

title: Bhishma's Vow
subtitle: Bhishma Pratigya
context: To secure his father's marriage, Bhishma vows lifelong celibacy and unconditional loyalty to the throne of Hastinapura. That oath later binds him to fight for the unjust Kauravas and sit silent through Draupadi's humiliation.
dilemma: Is a vow sacred even when it forces you to serve injustice?
cutsBothWays: Defends a person true to their word is utterly dependable. Warns rigid commitments can imprison conscience; rules must bend to justice.
modernParallel: An officer who hides behind 'rules are rules' while a citizen is plainly wronged.
quoteReady: "A vow kept against conscience is loyalty to the wrong master."

title: Draupadi's Court
subtitle: The silence of the elders
context: Draupadi is dragged and humiliated in open court. Bhishma, Drona, and the elders—wise and powerful—stay silent, hiding behind procedure and the king's authority. Their inaction enables the outrage as surely as the perpetrators do.
dilemma: Is the silent, 'neutral' bystander truly innocent?
cutsBothWays: Defends respecting hierarchy and due process has its place. Warns silence before injustice is complicity, not neutrality.
modernParallel: Senior officials who 'see nothing' while a subordinate harasses the vulnerable.
quoteReady: "The neutral bystander has already chosen the oppressor's side."

title: Vidura's Counsel
subtitle: Vidura Niti
context: Vidura, the wise prime minister, repeatedly warns the blind king Dhritarashtra against favouring Duryodhana and toward justice for the Pandavas—unwelcome, unheeded advice he gives anyway, out of duty to the realm rather than the ruler.
dilemma: Should an adviser tell the powerful what they need, or what they want, to hear?
cutsBothWays: Defends fearless, honest counsel is the adviser's first duty. Warns counsel must be candid yet constructive, not merely contrarian.
modernParallel: A civil servant who records honest written dissent on a flawed political decision.
quoteReady: "An adviser who only flatters is an enemy in a friend's seat."

title: Eklavya's Thumb
subtitle: The denied disciple
context: Self-taught and brilliant, the tribal Eklavya surpasses the princes. Guru Drona, bound to royal favour, demands his right thumb as 'fee'—crippling a low-born talent to protect Arjuna's supremacy. Eklavya gives it without complaint.
dilemma: Does merit deserve a fair field, regardless of birth?
cutsBothWays: Defends respect for a teacher and a given word is admirable. Warns institutions must not sacrifice the marginalised to protect the privileged.
modernParallel: A gifted but poor candidate edged out so an insider's child can rise.
quoteReady: "Where the gifted must pay for their gift, justice has already lost."

title: Abhimanyu & the Chakravyuha
subtitle: Sent in half-prepared
context: Young Abhimanyu knows how to enter the deadly Chakravyuha formation but not how to exit. Sent in anyway to break the enemy, he fights heroically, is surrounded and killed—undone by knowledge his elders left incomplete.
dilemma: Who is at fault when the willing are sent in unprepared?
cutsBothWays: Defends courage and willingness to take on hard tasks are vital. Warns leaders must equip people fully, not exploit their eagerness.
modernParallel: A junior pushed into a crisis posting without training, then blamed when it fails.
quoteReady: "Courage without preparation is a debt the leader owes, not the led."

title: Krishna's Peace Mission
subtitle: The last embassy
context: Before the war, Krishna travels to the Kaurava court seeking peace, asking only for five villages for the Pandavas. Only when every avenue of negotiation is exhausted and contemptuously refused does war become unavoidable.
dilemma: When is force justified, and have we truly tried everything else first?
cutsBothWays: Defends exhausting peaceful means before force is the mark of the just. Warns endless talks with bad faith can themselves enable injustice.
modernParallel: An administration that exhausts dialogue with protesters before any coercive step.
quoteReady: "Force earns legitimacy only after peace is honestly attempted."

title: Yaksha Prashna
subtitle: Questions at the lake
context: A lake-spirit bars the thirsty Yudhishthira until he answers riddles. Asked the greatest wonder of the world, he replies: that men see others die each day, yet each believes himself immortal—a meditation on folly and perspective.
dilemma: What blinds capable people to obvious truths about themselves?
cutsBothWays: Defends self-knowledge and reflection are the root of wisdom. Warns endless introspection must still translate into action.
modernParallel: A leader who plans for everyone's risks but never their own blind spots.
quoteReady: "The rarest wisdom is to see oneself as clearly as one sees others."

title: Yudhishthira & the Dog
subtitle: The climb to heaven
context: On the final journey, all of Yudhishthira's companions fall away but a stray dog. At heaven's gate, he is offered entry—but not the dog. He refuses heaven itself rather than abandon the faithful, voiceless creature that trusted him.
dilemma: Do our principles hold when keeping them costs us the prize?
cutsBothWays: Defends loyalty to the helpless, even at great cost, is true virtue. Warns even noble stubbornness must check that it isn't mere pride.
modernParallel: An officer who won't drop the weakest beneficiary just to hit an easy target.
quoteReady: "A heaven bought by abandoning the faithful is not worth entering."

title: Rama Accepts Exile
subtitle: Honouring a father's word
context: On the eve of his coronation, Rama is exiled for fourteen years to honour a promise his father made to Kaikeyi. He accepts without resentment, placing duty and his father's word above his own throne and comfort.
dilemma: How far should one sacrifice for duty and a promise made by another?
cutsBothWays: Defends honouring one's word and elders builds a society of trust. Warns blind obedience can also perpetuate an unjust demand.
modernParallel: An official who honours a prior, inconvenient commitment rather than wriggle out.
quoteReady: "A promise kept at personal cost is the currency of public trust."

title: Sita's Banishment
subtitle: Ruler vs rumour
context: To quiet a washerman's gossip about Sita's 'purity', King Rama banishes his innocent, pregnant queen—privileging the public perception of the throne over fairness to a person who had done no wrong.
dilemma: Should a ruler bow to public opinion at the cost of an individual's justice?
cutsBothWays: Defends a leader must guard public confidence in the institution. Warns sacrificing an innocent to appease opinion is injustice, not duty.
modernParallel: An administration that suspends an innocent officer to 'manage optics' after a rumour.
quoteReady: "When optics outrank the innocent, justice is outsourced to the mob."

title: Bharata's Padukas
subtitle: The regent who would not reign
context: Offered the throne in Rama's absence, Bharata refuses to rule in his own name. He places Rama's sandals on the throne and governs only as a caretaker, holding the kingdom in trust until the rightful king returns.
dilemma: Is power something you own, or something you merely hold for another?
cutsBothWays: Defends treating office as a trust, not a possession, is model integrity. Warns a caretaker must still govern decisively, not merely keep the seat warm.
modernParallel: An acting head who takes no irreversible favours and hands over cleanly.
quoteReady: "He ruled best who never forgot the chair was not his."

title: Vibhishana's Defection
subtitle: Conscience over kin
context: Ravana's own brother Vibhishana counsels him to return the abducted Sita and avoid ruin. Spurned and insulted, he finally leaves Ravana's side to join Rama—choosing justice over blood, at the price of being called a traitor.
dilemma: Is leaving a wrongful cause loyalty to a higher principle, or betrayal?
cutsBothWays: Defends conscience can rightly override loyalty to family or boss. Warns 'principled exit' can also be self-serving desertion—motive matters.
modernParallel: A whistle-blower who exposes their own organisation's serious wrongdoing.
quoteReady: "To leave a sinking wrong is not desertion—it is allegiance to the right."

title: Hanuman's Service
subtitle: Strength without ego
context: Immensely powerful yet utterly humble, Hanuman places all his strength at the service of Rama's cause, seeking neither reward nor recognition—often forgetting his own might until reminded. Power held lightly, used wholly for others.
dilemma: Can great capacity stay free of ego and self-interest?
cutsBothWays: Defends strength devoted selflessly to a cause is the ideal of service. Warns service must stay thoughtful, not blind devotion to a person.
modernParallel: A brilliant officer content to make the system shine rather than themselves.
quoteReady: "True strength forgets itself in the work it serves."

title: Jatayu's Stand
subtitle: The doomed defender
context: The aged vulture Jatayu sees Ravana abducting Sita and, though far outmatched, fights to stop him. Mortally wounded, he delays Ravana and lives just long enough to tell Rama the direction taken—doing right though certain to lose.
dilemma: Should you act for the right even when defeat is certain?
cutsBothWays: Defends courage to resist wrong regardless of outcome is the highest duty. Warns valour must be weighed against futile, avoidable sacrifice.
modernParallel: An honest official who flags grand corruption knowing it may cost their career.
quoteReady: "He lost the fight and won the only thing that mattered—his duty."

title: Ravana
subtitle: The learned tyrant
context: Ravana is no fool—a great scholar, devotee, and king. Yet his immense learning and power, unmoored from self-restraint and respect for others, curdle into arrogance and the abduction that destroys him and his kingdom.
dilemma: What is brilliance worth without the character to govern it?
cutsBothWays: Defends competence, scholarship, and strength are genuine goods. Warns ability without ethics becomes the most dangerous force of all.
modernParallel: A highly capable officer whose talent serves only ambition and ego.
quoteReady: "Knowledge without character is a sharpened blade in a careless hand."

title: Shabari's Berries
subtitle: Devotion over ritual
context: The humble, low-born forest-dweller Shabari offers Rama berries she has first tasted to ensure they are sweet—a breach of 'purity' custom. Rama eats them gladly, honouring the love behind the gift over caste and ritual form.
dilemma: Does the spirit of an act matter more than its ritual form or the giver's status?
cutsBothWays: Defends sincerity and inclusion outrank empty formalism and hierarchy. Warns form and procedure still protect fairness; don't discard them wholesale.
modernParallel: An officer who values a poor citizen's genuine plea over rigid procedural propriety.
quoteReady: "Heaven tasted the love, not the ritual, in Shabari's berries."

title: Kaikeyi & Manthara
subtitle: The poison of counsel
context: Kaikeyi loves Rama—until her maid Manthara drips suspicion and ambition into her ear, twisting a good queen into demanding his exile. A single manipulative adviser reshapes the fate of a kingdom.
dilemma: How responsible are we for the counsel we absorb—and the counsel we give?
cutsBothWays: Defends loyalty to one's ward can look like devoted care. Warns those who whisper self-serving counsel bear real moral weight.
modernParallel: An aide who manipulates a decision-maker on behalf of a vested interest.
quoteReady: "Power is rarely lost to enemies; more often it is whispered away."

title: Prahlada
subtitle: Faith against the father-king
context: The boy Prahlada refuses to abandon his devotion despite the escalating threats of his tyrant father Hiranyakashipu, who has banned all worship but of himself. Prahlada holds to conscience against absolute, even parental, authority.
dilemma: Must conscience yield to authority, even a parent's or a ruler's command?
cutsBothWays: Defends standing by conscience against unjust power is moral courage. Warns defiance must rest on principle, not mere rebellion.
modernParallel: An official who refuses an illegal order issued from the very top.
quoteReady: "No throne is high enough to command a clear conscience."

title: Harishchandra
subtitle: Truth through ruin
context: To keep a promise, King Harishchandra surrenders his kingdom, sells his wife and son, and becomes a cremation-ground attendant—refusing at every step to lie or break his word, even as his world collapses around him.
dilemma: Is truthfulness worth any price, or can it become its own kind of pride?
cutsBothWays: Defends unbreakable honesty is the bedrock of all trust. Warns honesty pursued blindly can harm dependents one also owes a duty.
modernParallel: An officer who discloses an uncomfortable truth though it sinks their own project.
quoteReady: "He lost everything he had and kept the one thing he was—true."

title: Shravan Kumar
subtitle: The devoted son
context: Shravan Kumar carries his old, blind parents on a pilgrimage in slings across his shoulders, tending to their every need. His life embodies selfless care for elders and the quiet ethics of everyday duty.
dilemma: What do we owe those who once cared for us?
cutsBothWays: Defends devoted care for elders and dependents is a foundational duty. Warns care for one's own must not crowd out wider duties to society.
modernParallel: An officer who builds genuine eldercare into welfare rather than as an afterthought.
quoteReady: "A society is judged by how it carries those who once carried it."

title: Bali & Vamana
subtitle: The king who kept his word
context: The generous king Bali promises the dwarf Vamana as much land as he can cover in three steps. Vamana grows cosmic and covers earth and heaven in two; Bali, true to his word, offers his own head for the third—honour over self-preservation.
dilemma: Does keeping a promise hold even when you realise you were outmanoeuvred?
cutsBothWays: Defends honouring a given word, even at ruin, is supreme integrity. Warns blind, unconditional promising can be exploited—diligence matters.
modernParallel: An officer who honours a signed commitment despite finding it a bad bargain.
quoteReady: "His word outweighed his crown—and that made him the greater king."

title: The Churning of the Ocean
subtitle: The one who drank the poison
context: As gods and demons churn the ocean for nectar, a lethal poison emerges first, threatening all. Shiva drinks it to save creation, holding it in his throat—the leader who absorbs the worst so that others may share the good.
dilemma: Who should bear the cost when a shared venture throws up shared danger?
cutsBothWays: Defends leaders earn trust by absorbing the hardest burdens themselves. Warns heroic sacrifice shouldn't excuse poor planning that created the risk.
modernParallel: A chief who takes responsibility for a failure rather than scapegoating juniors.
quoteReady: "The mark of a leader is to swallow the poison and pass on the nectar."

title: Nachiketa
subtitle: Knowledge over reward
context: Offered wealth, long life, and every pleasure by Yama, lord of death, the boy Nachiketa refuses them all, insisting instead on the knowledge of the Self. He prizes truth over every inducement and questions fearlessly.
dilemma: When offered every inducement to look away, do we still seek the truth?
cutsBothWays: Defends valuing principle over inducements is rare integrity. Warns idealism must still engage the practical needs of life.
modernParallel: An officer who turns down a lucrative, compromising offer to keep their independence.
quoteReady: "The wise are those no bribe can buy and no fear can bend."

title: Dadhichi's Bones
subtitle: The ultimate gift
context: When the gods need an invincible weapon to defeat a great evil, only the bones of the sage Dadhichi will serve. He willingly gives up his own life so his body can be forged into the vajra that saves the world.
dilemma: How much may be asked of one person for the good of all?
cutsBothWays: Defends supreme self-sacrifice for the common good is the noblest act. Warns a just society should not routinely demand martyrdom of its best.
modernParallel: A frontline worker who risks everything in a disaster so that others survive.
quoteReady: "Some give their time, some their comfort; the rarest give their very selves."

title: Savitri & Satyavan
subtitle: Outwitting death
context: When Yama comes to claim her husband Satyavan, Savitri follows and engages him in such wise, principled argument that Yama, granting boon after boon, is finally outreasoned into restoring Satyavan's life. Determination joined to intelligence.
dilemma: Can reasoned persistence move even an immovable authority?
cutsBothWays: Defends principled persistence and persuasion achieve what force cannot. Warns persistence must stay honest argument, never manipulation.
modernParallel: An officer who wins a just outcome through dogged, reasoned advocacy.
quoteReady: "She argued where others would weep, and reasoned death into retreat."

title: The Righteous Butcher
subtitle: Dharmavyadha
context: A proud ascetic, humbled when his powers fail, is sent to learn dharma from a humble butcher who serves his parents and plies his lowly trade with honesty and devotion. Righteousness, the tale says, lives in how one does one's duty, not in one's status.
dilemma: Does virtue belong to a station, or to how one fills it?
cutsBothWays: Defends every honest occupation has dignity; character outranks rank. Warns diligence at a task still needs the right ends, not effort alone.
modernParallel: Valuing a sanitation worker's integrity as highly as a senior officer's rank.
quoteReady: "Dharma asks not what work you do, but how truly you do it."

title: Vishwamitra
subtitle: From king to sage
context: The warrior-king Vishwamitra, enraged at losing to the sage Vasishtha, undertakes vast penance to become a Brahmarshi. His pride and temper repeatedly undo him until he conquers his own ego—proving the hardest conquest is of oneself.
dilemma: Which is the harder victory—over others, or over oneself?
cutsBothWays: Defends relentless self-improvement and self-mastery are admirable. Warns ambition driven by ego and rivalry corrodes before it elevates.
modernParallel: A talented officer whose career turns on learning to govern their own temper.
quoteReady: "He conquered kingdoms easily; conquering himself took a lifetime."

title: Prometheus's Fire
subtitle: Altruistic rebellion
context: Prometheus steals fire from Mount Olympus to give to humanity, empowering them with warmth, technology, and civilization. In doing so, he deliberately violates Zeus's supreme decree, fully aware of his eternal punishment of being chained to a rock.
dilemma: Should one break a supreme authority's law to relieve the suffering of others?
cutsBothWays: Defends self-sacrifice for human progress is the ultimate virtue. Warns lawlessness and violating authority can invite systemic chaos.
modernParallel: A whistleblower leaking confidential corporate safety leaks to protect local public health.
quoteReady: "The fire of human progress is worth the chains of divine tyranny."

title: Pandora's Box
subtitle: Reckless curiosity
context: Pandora is given a wedding jar containing all the world's sickness, misery, and evils, with strict instructions never to open it. Driven by curiosity, she lifts the lid, unleashing eternal plagues upon mankind, leaving only Hope trapped at the bottom.
dilemma: Is the pursuit of curiosity or knowledge worth releasing unpredictable, catastrophic risks?
cutsBothWays: Defends natural curiosity drives human discovery. Warns actions taken without foresight can unleash irreversible systemic damage.
modernParallel: Deploying advanced generative AI algorithms or biological engineering without guardrails.
quoteReady: "Once opened, the container of unintended consequences can never be resealed."

title: Sisyphus's Boulder
subtitle: Finding meaning in futility
context: Condemned by the gods for outwitting them, Sisyphus must roll a massive boulder up a steep mountain, only to watch it roll back down to the valley under its own weight, repeating this task for eternity. He must find purpose in the struggle.
dilemma: Can one find dignity and purpose in repetitive, seemingly futile labor?
cutsBothWays: Defends persistent resilience is a victory of human spirit. Warns monotonous, unproductive labor drains human motivation.
modernParallel: A public servant working within a highly corrupt system, persistently reforming small forms.
quoteReady: "The struggle itself toward the heights is enough to fill a man's heart."

title: Icarus's Flight
subtitle: The melt of ambition
context: Escaping from a labyrinth using wings made of wax and feathers, Icarus ignores his father Daedalus's warning to fly a middle course. Thrilled by flight, he climbs too close to the sun, melting the wax, and falls to his death.
dilemma: Should we limit ambition and aspiration to guarantee safety and stability?
cutsBothWays: Defends daring exploration expands human capability. Warns unbridled ego and ignoring limits leads to catastrophic failure.
modernParallel: A fast-rising executive taking high-risk shortcuts to achieve targets, causing bankruptcy.
quoteReady: "He who flies too high melts the wings of his own success."

title: The Judgment of Solomon
subtitle: The true mother's sacrifice
context: Two women claim the same infant. King Solomon orders the child to be cut in half, offering one part to each. The real mother immediately relinquishes her claim to the child to preserve its life, exposing the false mother's greed.
dilemma: How should a judge identify truth when witnesses lie and evidence is identical?
cutsBothWays: Defends unorthodox psychological insight can reveal truth. Warns extreme bluffs could cause fatal outcomes if poorly executed.
modernParallel: A dispute arbitrator using tactical pressure to force a compromise between rival agencies.
quoteReady: "True justice protects life, even at the expense of legal ownership."

title: Odin's Eye
subtitle: Sacrifice for foresight
context: Odin seeks to drink from Mimir's Well of Wisdom to protect his realm from Ragnarok. The guardian Mimir demands his right eye as price. Odin gouges his own eye out and drops it in the well, buying foresight with physical loss.
dilemma: Is personal sacrifice of vital capability justified to acquire foresight for the public good?
cutsBothWays: Defends leaders must bear personal loss to guide their organizations. Warns self-impairment can limit effectiveness in future battles.
modernParallel: An administrator sacrificing health or family well-being to acquire specialist skills.
quoteReady: "True vision is bought with the eye of personal sacrifice."

title: Damocles's Sword
subtitle: The thread of power
context: Damocles envies the wealth and power of King Dionysius. The king invites him to a lavish banquet, but suspends a razor-sharp sword directly above his seat, held only by a single strand of horsehair, showing him the constant terror of rule.
dilemma: Is the authority and prestige of leadership worth the constant threat of ruin?
cutsBothWays: Defends authority enables large-scale public reforms. Warns high office demands constant vulnerability and systemic anxiety.
modernParallel: A director managing a massive developmental budget under threat of audits and public attacks.
quoteReady: "The crown of power hangs beneath the sword of vulnerability."

title: The Ring of Gyges
subtitle: Ethics in the dark
context: A shepherd discovers a ring that makes him invisible. Free from the fear of detection or consequences, he sneaks into the palace, seduces the queen, murders the king, and usurps the throne, proving that external checks enforce morality.
dilemma: Would a person act ethically if they were completely invisible and faced zero consequences?
cutsBothWays: Defends true integrity must be self-imposed. Warns without accountability, absolute power corrupts.
modernParallel: Anonymous cyber-actors using untraceable tools to manipulate public registers.
quoteReady: "Character is what a man is in the dark, when invisible to the world."

title: Achilles's Heel
subtitle: The single vulnerability
context: Dipped in the River Styx by his mother to grant him invulnerability, the warrior Achilles is protected everywhere except his heel, where his mother held him. He dies when an arrow strikes that single vulnerable spot during the Trojan War.
dilemma: Can supreme strength coexist with a single critical vulnerability, and how should it be managed?
cutsBothWays: Defends unmatched excellence can win crucial battles. Warns neglecting a single flaw leaves the entire structure exposed to defeat.
modernParallel: A state-of-the-art secure digital banking platform compromised by a single weak password.
quoteReady: "The greatest armor is only as strong as its weakest seam."

title: The Trojan Horse
subtitle: Strategic deceit
context: Unable to breach the walls of Troy, the Greeks build a hollow wooden horse as a fake peace offering. The Trojans drag it inside. That night, Greek soldiers emerge and sack the city, winning a decade-long war through deceit.
dilemma: Is deception justified to resolve a prolonged, high-stakes stalemated conflict?
cutsBothWays: Defends clever strategy can resolve conflict and prevent further blood loss. Warns deceptive tactics destroy the trust needed for future peace.
modernParallel: Deploying undercover audits inside an agency under the guise of an advisory training program.
quoteReady: "Beware of adversaries bearing gifts."
